title: “ Qanon”是宣传，我们知道谁负责
title_en: “Qanon” is Propaganda, and we know who’s responsible

note:
现代社交媒体基础设施中的根本缺陷已不再是新鲜事物，使用区块链和其他去中心化技术来对其进行补救的想法也不是什么。但是，这些问题从未像现在这样突出。从来没有像现在这样的人如此大地意识到Big Tech控制我们通信的系统核心所在的道德和实践缺陷。
 苹果，谷歌，推特，脸书，亚马逊和其他公司最近采取行动打击这种被认为鼓舞右翼反政府暴力的在线讨论，其速度和魅力仍然使美国人感到震惊。 Twitter等高调行动暂停了美国总统特朗普的帐户，苹果，谷歌和亚马逊同时取消了Parler的平台，引起了所有人的关注。同样令人不安的是，如果没有删除Google令人反感的内容，Google威胁要从Android Play商店中删除Facebook Alternatives.com。
  头脑仓促地保留了Play网站上的部分内容（例如评论功能），从而留在了Play商店。而且，它绝对不是一个面向ALTA，面向MAGA的社交网络。创立minds.com的比尔·奥特曼（Bill Ottman）显然处于左翼倾向，尽管他将个人政治排除在公司运营之外。除了很多精彩的内容之外，Minds.com上确实有一些讨厌的评论，而且QAnon的读者比我想读的更多。但是，就像在Facebook上一样，我不需要去那些页面，除非被一堆奇怪的好奇心吸引住了。
 当然，互联网上也有很多令人讨厌的内容和许多QAnon BS，您可以使用Google的搜索引擎轻松找到它们。为什么Google认为将搜索者指向QAnon页面在道德上是可以的，但又因为其QAnon会员人数很少而感到与minds.com发生冲突的“道德”需求？
  人们可以在某种程度上同那些经营这些大技术公司的人同情。他们处于一种典型的“如果你不喜欢就该死，如果你不喜欢就该死”的情况，在他们变得如此庞大之前，他们都没有预见到。
 通过注册，您将收到有关CoinDesk产品的电子邮件，并且您同意我们的条款＆amp;条件和隐私政策。
 如果他们允许令人反感的内容或人们以一种不太非法但相当讨厌的方式争论暴力行为，那么当事实证明他们所托管的内容在某些实际的恐怖行为中发挥了作用时，他们将获得反击。 
如果他们开始禁止内容，那么他​​们将不可避免地禁止实际上并不非常危险的内容，突然之间他们便成为言论自由的邪恶反对者。
 鉴于情况和与之打交道的人的数量，这些公司如何才能始终如一地找到正确的划界线，尤其是当整个社会对划界线没有统一或一致的想法时。
  Twitter，通常是Big Tech巨头中最具前瞻性的人，已经任命了一个小团队来开发去中心化的社交媒体协议。显然，该想法是使Twitter使用通用的标准协议成为多种服务之一，并将有争议的问题（如内容审核和禁止）从Twitter层移到分散协议层。这一愿望令人钦佩，但目前尚不清楚，Twitter业务模型的基本集中式逻辑将如何与所需的根本分权程度相协调。
 我自己的强烈感觉是：Twitter不会尽其所能解决这个问题。集中化的大技术世界不会解决这个问题。政府也不会解决这个问题。它的步调太慢，太钝了，无法处理在人文和技术方面都高度复杂的快速发展的问题，并且与西方宪法和商法的精妙之处相适应。
  中国大陆政府以自己的方式“解决”了大技术和社交媒体的问题：通过施加明确和详细的政府控制。在现代中国语境下，这很好用，但这仅仅是因为这种语境不涉及西方人对言论自由或业务自主权的期望。如果您是中国大陆的技术首席执行官，则无需费心思索是否符合道德标准。您只需要接受政府的指导。在其他方面，充其量您最终会像马云一样，放下一会儿，然后将重点转移到慈善事业上。
 好消息是：切实可行的解决西方社会社交媒体问题的方法的要素是众所周知的，而并不是难以置信的神秘。
  更复杂的消息是：区块链社区知道如何解决这些问题，但它远没有像Big Tech那样资源丰富，也不是政府的宠儿，并且拥有更好的往绩记录，为精英极客们打造了很棒的技术比那些为普通TikTok用户带来温暖的应用的应用程序。 
我们最近看到了另一个严重的社会问题的例子，其中区块链社区掌握了许多关键因素：COVID-19大流行。区块链技术是量身定制的，可提供对COVID-19感染的安全匿名跟踪。
 从技术上讲，没有充分的理由让每个拥有智能手机的西方公民都没有在手机上安装基于区块链的COVID-19跟踪应用程序。这些应用程序可以使用安全的分散式AI分析个人医疗数据，并将每个人的数据输入到在分散式平台上运行的基于代理的流行病学模拟模型中。
 如果发生这种情况，美国，西欧和拉丁美洲的COVID-19可能会像亚洲国家一样迅速衰落（在亚洲，使用隐私保护程度较低的跟踪和追踪应用程序倾向于被社会接受）。如果区块链在保护隐私的同时帮助西方躲避COVID-19发挥了关键作用，那将是去中心化技术的关键时刻。它会以一种奇妙的方式证明，除了怪异的金融赌博和洗钱活动外，它还对其他方面有好处。
  使用区块链帮助COVID-19的障碍主要不是技术上的。实际上，在2020年期间，使用安全的去中心化技术创建了许多有趣的软件工具，以进行跟踪，个人健康评估，流行病学建模等等。但是到目前为止，此类软件的使用量很少。这是一个千载难逢的机会。
 去中心化社交媒体是一个更大，更关键的机会。与COVID-19大流行一样悲惨，但很快就会被疫苗抑制。即将出现的设计不良的社交媒体网络没有疫苗。由大型技术控制的社交媒体对人类的负面影响具有比冠状病毒大流行更大的破坏力。
 有一个正当的论点，即大科技社交媒体通过煽动仇恨和部落主义的火焰，可以大大增加人类被歼灭的几率。
  像尼克·博斯特罗姆（Nick Bostrom）这样的学术b徒担心超人AGI会重新利用人类分子来增加其硬盘的额外存储容量。但是，Big Tech使用狭machine的机器学习算法来最大程度地提高股东价值，却以牺牲人类福祉，智慧和同情心为代价，存在着更大的风险。 
创建更好的社交网络所需的基本原理并不难理解：
 开源代码。代码应该是开源的，因此每个人都有能力检查和理解将图像和视频摆在他们的眼前，将人们放入他们的友谊圈，并将文本和思想放入他们的思想的算法。
 分散控制。所有权和控制权应下放，因此没有公司和政府承担任何责任和负担。
 民主的决定。有关社交网络结构和动态的重大决定应民主做出。而且，如果民主决策不能最终达成共识，那么社交网络可以分裂为多个，就像Linux，比特币和以太坊已经形成了多个版本一样。
 可解释的AI。对连接和内容的建议应使用通过民主管理的项目创建的开源AI提出，并且应该能够解释其判断背后的原因。这些说明必须以用户可以理解的方式例行显示给用户。
 体现这些原理的技术目前处于成熟状态，尽管处于不同的成熟水平。
 在SingularityNET中，我们一直在研究基于区块链的AI工具，这些工具适合用作去中心化社交媒体网络的认知核心。您的社交媒体和Internet使用数据应归您所有和控制，用于建模此数据的AI算法应在您的控制之下。而且您应该能够检查和询问这些模型，并了解他们对您的想法以及为什么他们向您而不是其他人推荐某些东西。 
应用于人的社交媒体和互联网使用数据的透明，可解释的AI可能会成为自我理解和自我成长的强大工具。看着我做的事情的AI很可能会比我更了解自己的某些方面，这对我很感兴趣，因为我想更好地了解自己。
 可以轻松想象一个更聪明，更透明的Alexa或Google助手版本，该版本旨在用作“ AI媒体导航器”，以一种精明而公正的方式找到我所要的内容，并向我推荐它真正认为的内容和内容我感兴趣的但是，这种远景与当前现实大相径庭，在现实中，各种由大型高科技公司控制的AI监视，建模和指导我的目的是挤钱，并让我尽可能长时间盯着某些特定的应用程序或网站。
   有人可能会说，企业控制我们的思想，心智和银行账户就是我们为大技术公司用来向我们提供社交媒体帖子的日常饮食所具有的所有宝贵处理能力所付出的代价。但这是道德和技术想象力的又一次失败。基于分散的计算能力和分散的算法提供现代社交媒体功能完全在我们当前的能力范围内。多年来，在Golem，SONM和CPUCoin等项目中，这一直是区块链领域的主题。为此，SingularityNET一直在孵化NuNet.io项目。
 所有这些去中心化处理能力的初创公司都需要一些成熟才能满足Facebook，Twitter和Tiktok的去中心化类似物的需求。但是这里有基本的算法和体系结构。如果分散，民主控制的处理基础架构获得了集中式服务器场基础架构所拥有的财务和软件开发火力的十分之一呢？
 有了社交媒体的分散基础架构，从这个或那个社交网络禁止这个或那个人或话题的整个问题将基本上消失。
 去中心化，透明操作，安全的AI驱动的信誉系统将对帖子和张贴者进行排名和评分，从而形成一个系统，在这些系统中，当他们发现令人反感或令人不安的帖子时，就无需发生任何事情。
 另一方面，如果您不想继续迷恋熟悉的社区和思想领域，并想冒接触新事物和潜在麻烦的风险，也可以将此事告知AI导航员。 
当然，有些人会使用去中心化社交媒体来共同计划犯罪。但是人们也出于这种目的而聚集在公园或酒吧中，我们不能通过禁止在政府麦克风的主持下进行小组面对面的交谈来解决这一问题。我毫不怀疑，与以前的技术创新一样，执法活动将出现在分散的社交网络中。
  毫无疑问，任何去中心化社交网络上的欺诈尝试都将猖ramp，而AI驱动的导航员将需要依靠复杂的“ AI信誉系统警察”来防范欺诈和尝试使用该系统。但关键点在于：AI监管应该在打击声誉系统欺诈的层面上，即通过假装人们假装其内容的案例来证明，这不是在捣乱内容本身。
 让我们不要忘记，除了所有的仇恨和愚蠢之外，每天每天都有大量的绝妙想法和奇妙的创意涌入互联网。由于当今集中的在线信息架构，其中大多数都很难找到他们应得的受众。
 我们拥有核心技术，可以从根本上实现更具道德和效益的社交媒体网络。剩下的就是使这项技术脱离区块链贫民窟，并进入互联网主流。这不是一个小任务，但潜在的好处是巨大的。我们在这里所说的不仅仅是在软件行业的某个领域的一场革命。这是对集体思维和人类内心如何指导自身发展的一次重大升级。就我个人而言，我为处于如此巨大而有潜力实现如此深刻而广泛的积极转型的技术生态系统而感到兴奋和清醒。
   披露CoinDesk是区块链新闻的领导者，是一家致力于追求最高新闻标准并遵守一套严格的编辑政策的媒体机构。 CoinDesk是Digital Currency Group的独立运营子公司，该公司投资于加密货币和区块链初创公司。
note_en:
The fundamental flaws in modern social media infrastructure are nothing new, and nor is the idea of using blockchain and other decentralized technologies to help remedy them. But never before have these issues risen to the fore as they are currently. Never before have so many people been so aware of the ethical and practical weaknesses at the heart of Big Tech’s systems for controlling our communications.
 The American people’s collective jaws are still somewhat agape at the speed and oomph with which Apple, Google, Twitter, Facebook, Amazon and others have recently acted to combat online discussion perceived as encouraging right-wing anti-government violence. High-profile actions like Twitter suspending U.S. President Trump’s account, and Apple and Google and Amazon concurrently deplatforming Parler got everyone’s attention. Just as disturbing was Google threatening to remove Facebook alternative  minds.com from the Android Play Store if it didn’t remove content Google found offensive.
  Minds managed to stay on the Play Store by hastily gutting parts of its site like the commenting functionality. And it’s definitely not an alt-right, MAGA-oriented social network. Bill Ottman, who founded minds.com, is pretty clearly left-wing in orientation, though he keeps his personal politics out of his company’s operations. Along with a lot of fantastic content, Minds.com does have some nasty commentary on it, and more QAnon bulls**t than I would ever want to read. But, just as on Facebook, I don’t need to go to those pages except when struck by a particular bout of perverse curiosity.
 And the internet, of course, also has a lot of nasty stuff and a lot of QAnon BS on it, which one can readily find using Google’s search engine. Why is it that Google considers it ethically okay to point searchers to QAnon pages, but also feels an “ethical” need to screw with minds.com because it has a minority population of QAnon members?
  One can sympathize to some degree with the folks who run these Big Tech companies. They’re in a classic “damned if you do, damned if you don’t” situation, which none of them foresaw before they got so huge.
 By signing up, you will receive emails about CoinDesk products and you agree to our  terms & conditions and  privacy policy.
 If they allow offensive content or folks arguing for violence in a not-quite-illegal-but-pretty-nasty way, then they’ll get blowback when it turns out content they hosted played a role in some actual horrible acts.
  If they start banning content, inevitably they’re going to ban stuff that isn’t actually extraordinarily dangerous, and suddenly they’re evil opponents of free speech.
 And with the volume of situations and people they’re dealing with, how can these companies consistently figure out the right place to draw the line, especially when society as a whole doesn’t have a consistent or coherent idea of where to draw the line.
  Twitter, generally the most forward-thinking of the Big Tech titans, has  appointed a small team to develop a decentralized social media protocol. The idea, apparently, is to make Twitter one among multiple services using a common standard protocol, and to move controversial issues like content moderation and banning from the Twitter layer down to the decentralized protocol layer. The aspiration is admirable but at the present time it’s not at all clear how the fundamentally centralized logic of the Twitter business model is going to harmonize with the radical degree of decentralization that would be needed.
 My own strong feeling is: Twitter is not going to solve this, as good as its intentions may be. The centralized Big Tech world is not going to solve this. Government is not going to solve this either. It’s far too slow-paced and obtuse to deal with fast-evolving issues that are highly complex in both human and technological dimensions, in a way that’s compatible with the subtleties of Western constitutional and business law.
  The mainland Chinese government has “solved” the issue of Big Tech and social media in its own way: by exerting explicit and detailed government control. This works fine in the modern Chinese context, but only because this context doesn’t involve Western-style expectations of free speech or business autonomy. If you’re a mainland Chinese tech CEO, you don’t need to do a lot of soul-searching about what’s ethical or not. You just have to accept government guidance. Elsewise, at best you’ll end up  like Jack Ma, laying low for a while and then shifting your focus to philanthropy.
 The good news is: The elements of a truly workable solution to Western society’s social media problem are reasonably well known and not incredibly mysterious.
  The more complex news is this: The blockchain community knows how to fix these problems, but it is not remotely as well-resourced as Big Tech, is not exactly a darling of the government and has a better track record making cool tech for elite geeks than apps that give warm fuzzies to the average TikTok user.
  We have recently seen another example of an acute social problem where the blockchain community held many of the keys:  the COVID-19 pandemic. Blockchain technology is tailor-made to provide secure, anonymized tracking and tracing of COVID-19 infection.
 Technologically, there’s no good reason every Western citizen with a smartphone didn’t have a blockchain-based COVID-19 tracking and tracing app on their phone. These apps could have analyzed personal medical data using secure decentralized AI, and fed everyone’s data into agent-based epidemiological simulation models running on decentralized platforms.
 Had this happened, the U.S., Western Europe and Latin America would have likely squashed COVID-19 as rapidly as the Asian countries (where use of less privacy-preserving track-and-trace apps tended to be socially accepted). Had blockchain played a key role in helping the West dodge COVID-19 while preserving privacy, this would have been a pivotal moment for decentralized technology. It would have proved in a wonderful way that it’s good for something besides geeky financial gambling and money laundering.
  The obstacles to using blockchain to help with COVID-19 weren’t mainly technical. In fact, during 2020, an interesting variety of software tools were created using secure decentralized technologies to do track-and-trace, personal health evaluation, epidemiological modeling and a lot more. But the adoption of such software has been minimal so far. This was a golden opportunity lost.
 Decentralized social media is an even larger and more critical opportunity. As tragic as the COVID-19 pandemic has been, it’s soon going to be squelched by vaccines. There is no vaccine for badly designed social media networks on the horizon. The negative impact of Big Tech-controlled social media on humanity has far greater destructive potential than the coronavirus pandemic.
 There’s a decent argument that Big Tech social media, by fanning flames of hate and tribalism, could significantly increase the odds of the annihilation of the human race.
  Academic boffins like  Nick Bostrom are worried about superhuman AGI repurposing human molecules to add extra memory capacity to their hard drives. But there is far more evident risk in Big Tech’s use of narrow-minded machine learning algorithms to maximize shareholder value at the expense of human well-being, intelligence and compassion.
  The basic principles needed to create better social networks are not too hard to see:
 Open source code. Code should be open source, so everyone has the ability to inspect and understand the algorithms that are putting images and videos in front of their eyes, people into their friendship circles, and texts and ideas into their minds.
 Decentralized control. Ownership and control should be decentralized, so no company and no government is tasked and burdened with calling all the shots.
 Democratic decision. Major decisions about social network structure and dynamics should be made democratically. And if democratic decision-making doesn’t converge on a result, it’s okay if a social network splinters into multiple ones, just as Linux, Bitcoin and Ethereum have forked into multiple versions.
 Explainable AI. Recommendations of connections and content should be made using open-source AI that is created via democratically-governed projects, and that has the ability to explain the reasons behind its judgments. And these explanations need to be shown routinely to users in a way they can understand.
 The technologies to manifest these principles are here right now, albeit at varying levels of maturity.
 In SingularityNET, we have been working on blockchain-based AI tools suitable for serving as the cognitive core of decentralized social media networks.  Your social media and Internet usage data should be owned and controlled by you, and the AI algorithms used to model this data should be under your control. And you should be able to inspect and interrogate these models and understand what they are thinking about you and why they are recommending certain things to you and not others.
 Transparent, explainable AI applied to a person’s social media and Internet usage data has the potential to be a powerful tool for self-understanding and self-growth. An AI watching what I do will very likely understand some aspects of myself better than I do, and this is of interest to me, since I would like to better understand myself.
 It’s easy to imagine a smarter, more transparent version of Alexa or Google Assistant designed to serve as an “AI media navigator” finding me what I ask for in a savvy but unbiased way, and recommending me people and content that it genuinely thinks will be of interest to me. But this sort of vision is hilariously distant from the current reality in which the various Big Tech-controlled AIs are monitoring, modeling and guiding me with the objective of milking money and keeping me staring at certain particular apps or websites as long as possible.
   One could argue that corporate control of our minds, hearts and bank accounts is the price we pay for all the valuable processing power Big Tech companies are utilizing to deliver us our daily diet of social media posts. But this is one more  failure of moral and technological imagination. It is well within our current capability to deliver modern social media functions based on decentralized compute power as well as decentralized algorithms. This has been a theme in the blockchain world for years now, with projects like Golem, SONM and CPUCoin. SingularityNET has been incubating the  NuNet.io project toward this end.
 All of these decentralized-processing-power startups need some maturing to serve the needs of decentralized analogues of Facebook, Twitter and Tiktok. But the basic algorithms and architectures are there. What if decentralized, democratically controlled processing infrastructures had received even one tenth of the  financial and software-development firepower that centralized server farm infrastructure has?
 With a decentralized infrastructure for social media, the whole issue of banning this or that person or topic from this or that social network would basically disappear.
 Decentralized, transparently-operated, secure AI-driven reputation systems would rank and rate posts and posters, resulting in a system where nobody needs to happen upon posts they find offensive or disturbing.
 On the other hand, if you don’t want to remain ensconced in your familiar community and idea-sphere and want to take the risk of contact with something new and potentially troublesome, you can tell this to your AI navigator as well.
   For sure, some people will use decentralized social media to plan crimes together. But people also gather in parks or bars for such purposes, and we don’t solve this by banning group face-to-face conversations out of earshot of government microphones. I have no doubt law enforcement will rise to the occasion of decentralized social networks as it’s done with prior tech innovations.
  There is also no doubt fraud attempts will be rampant on any decentralized social network, and AI-driven navigators will need to rely on sophisticated “AI reputation system police” to militate against fraud and attempts to game the system. But the key point is this: The AI policing should be on the level of squashing reputation-system fraud, i.e. seeing through cases where people are pretending their content is something it’s not, rather than squashing content itself.
 Let us not forget that, alongside all the hate and stupidity, there is a massive influx of brilliant ideas and wonderful creations being put out there on the internet every minute of every day. And most of these have a hard time finding the audience they deserve because of today’s centralized online information architectures.
 We have the core tech to enable radically more ethical and beneficial social media networks. All that remains is to get this tech out of the blockchain ghetto and into the internet mainstream. It’s not a small task, but the potential benefit is huge. What we’re talking about here is not just a revolution in a certain sector of the software industry. It’s a massive upgrade in how the collective mind and heart of humanity guides its own growth. Personally I’m thrilled and a bit sobered to be in the midst of a tech ecosystem with so much potential to effect such deep and wide positive transformation.

CoinDesk is an independent operating subsidiary of  Digital Currency Group, which invests in  cryptocurrencies and blockchain  startups.

苦苦挣扎的零售商GameStop的股票今天奇怪地创出历史新高。但这不是因为索尼，微软和任天堂突然决定停止以数字方式出售他们的游戏。这并不是因为新的Funko Pops系列席卷了互联网的想象力。
    不，股价飙升至历史新高，因为一些机构投资者押注该公司倒闭，并且社交媒体上的一群业余爱好者决定称其虚张声势，并尝试在此过程中致富。
 随着视频游戏越来越数字化，GameStop一直在努力改造自己。现在，老牌的投资者和Reddit的当日交易者将为自己的未来而战，并使公司的股价在此过程中变得可笑。
  在视频游戏世界中，人们普遍认为，由于……的兴起，GameStop即将退出市场。
 阅读更多
 今年年初，GameStop的股票交易价格接近每股20美元。从那以后的几周内，它的价值翻了三倍多，达到今天最高点的73美元。彭博社报道：“ GameStop迄今1月份迄今已上涨174％，其平均每日滚动10天波动率达到了该股票交易近二十年来的最高水平。”
    正如Ars Technica在本周早些时候报道的那样，一些投资者在去年秋天花了空头对GameStop的股票做空，从而有效地推测了GameStop的价值被高估了并且会崩溃，这可能会使他们赚到很多钱。 
我不是财务专家。您也不是，最有可能的。但是“做空”的原理非常简单：您认为资产的价格会下跌，因此您借入资产，以当前价格出售，然后当价格下跌时，将其回购给任何人从那里借来的零钱。
 同时，人们在诸如华尔街赌注（Wallstreetbets，自称“ 4chan找到了彭博终端”之类）等次级债券上闲逛，而金融影响力领域TikTok（绰号为FinTok）开始把钱花在GameStop的长寿背后。像Robinhood这样的准入门槛低的交易应用程序，使得互联网主导的活动变得更加容易。
   “ [E]本质上，WallStreetBets上的人以及YouTube和TikTok的几位投资者早在一年前就猜测，如果他们以低价购买GameStop的股票，卖空者最终将被迫大量补仓， Vice将在本周出版的另一本出色的解释文章中写道。
  考虑到GameStop出现了数月的坏消息和不良财务报告，做空似乎是明智的选择。但是随后，正如Vice所指出的那样，Reddit金融界人士开始沉迷于他们认为GameStop实际上是一个巨大的投资机会。逻辑是基于已经有多少其他投资者卖空了它。就在今天，CNBC报道GameStop是“美国股票市场上最短的名字。”
 GameStop曾经是视频游戏零售领域的中心，现在正面临严重的麻烦。作为身体…
 阅读更多
  如果有人卖空股票，即卖出股票并给原始所有者一张欠条，然后原始所有者需要存货，他们需要通过购买更多股票来“弥补”空头。这有助于进一步提高股票价格，使其更有价值，并可能在每个人都争先恐后地从同一数量有限的股票库中回购时，使股票价格不断上涨。 
GameStop的卖空者之一是安德鲁·左（Andrew Left），因其在做空公司的声誉而被纽约时报称为“华尔街的赏金猎人”，他认为自己做空薄弱，并通过发表有关公司为何倒闭的研究来跟进。在某些情况下，指控完全欺诈。昨天，Left在YouTube上放了一个六分半钟的视频，说明了GameStop失败的原因。 WallStreetBets反过来组织了金融专家吉姆·克莱默（Jim Cramer）所说的“伏击”，在一次协调运动中加大了GameStop的股票“挤压”左派，迫使他购买大量自己的股票来“掩盖”自己的头寸，进而做出他们的股份价值更高。吉姆·克莱默（Jim Cramer）绝对是个傻瓜，但如果有什么适合“疯狂的钱”，那就是这个。
  自昨天以来，Left的研究帐户Citron尚未在Twitter上发布。 GameStop投资者关系模仿帐户今天发推文说：“吸，@ CitronResearch。”
  那么，GameStop的当前库存状况实际上与其长期前景相比没有什么关系，而只是混乱的业余日间交易者加剧了市场中的现有怪异现象。那些看好GameStop命运的人中，有些人可能正确地嗅到了卖空者对该公司的未来过于消极。与互联网上的所有内容一样，随着某些人试图（并成功）赚到一大笔快钱而开始的事情已经变得越来越多，包括对左派的十字军东征以及GameStop狂热分子的不太可能的来源。
 本月初，GameStop宣布瑞安·科恩（Ryan Cohen）将担任董事会成员。科恩（Cohen）曾是宠物食品网站Chewy.com的首席执行官，但他已经成为GameStop股票界的黄金男孩。在Twitter上搜索他的名字，您会发现人们在发推文，例如“帕帕·科恩（Papa Cohen）会带我们去火星服役，以[火箭表情符号]颠簸起伏，所以您不会摔倒。”
  很难知道其中有多少是真正的个性崇拜，有多少是互联网讽刺，但这可以说明GameStop目前正处于一个奇怪的时刻，以及造成它的事件之间不太可能的融合。
note_en:
Struggling retailer GameStop’s stock curiously hit an all time high today. But it’s not because Sony, Microsoft, and Nintendo suddenly decided to stop selling their games digitally. And it’s not because a new set of Funko Pops has taken the internet’s imagination by storm.
    No, the stock price jumped to an all-time high because some institutional investors bet on the company to fail, and a bunch of amateurs on social media decided to call their bluff and try getting rich in the process.
 GameStop has struggled to reinvent itself as video games have increasingly gone digital. Now, established investors and Reddit day-traders are going to war over its future, and making the company’s stock price do ridiculous things in the process.
  Common wisdom in the video game world is that GameStop is on its way out thanks to the rise of…
 Read more
 At the beginning of the year, GameStop’s stock was trading at just under $20 a share. In the weeks since, it’s more than tripled in value reaching just over $73 at its highest point today. “GameStop is up 174% in January to date, with its average daily rolling 10-day volatility peaking at the highest level in the nearly two decades the stock has been trading,”    Bloomberg reported.
    As  Ars Technica   reported earlier this week, some investors spent last fall shorting GameStop’s stock, effectively speculating that it was overvalued and would implode, possibly making them a bunch of money in the process.
  I am not a finance expert. Neither are you, most likely. But the principle of “shorting” is pretty straightforward: you think the price of an asset will go down so you borrow it, sell it at the current price, and then when the price goes down, buy it back to give it to whoever you borrowed it from and pocket the difference.
 Meanwhile, people hanging out on subreddits   like Wallstreetbets (self-described as “Like 4chan found a Bloomberg Terminal”) and the   finance influencer realm of TikTok (nicknamed FinTok) started putting their money behind GameStop’s longevity. The internet-led campaign was made easier by low barrier-to-entry trading apps like Robinhood.
   “[E]ssentially, people on WallStreetBets, along with several YouTube and TikTok investors guessed as long as a year ago that if they bought shares of GameStop at a low price, the short sellers would eventually be forced to cover their short en masse, which would drive the price up,” wrote  Vice in   another great explainer published this week.
  Shorting seemed like a sensible bet considering   months of bad news and poor financial reports coming out of GameStop. But then, as    Vice pointed out, Reddit finance personalities began musing about how they thought GameStop was actually a great investment opportunity. The logic was based on how many other investors were already short selling it. Just today,    CNBC reported that GameStop is “the single most shorted name in the U.S. stock market.”
 GameStop, once the center of the video game retail universe, is in serious trouble. As physical…
 Read more
  If someone shorts a stock, i.e. sells it and gives the original owner an IOU, and then that original owner needs the stock back, they need to “cover” the short by buying additional stock. This helps pump up the price of the stock even further, making it more valuable and potentially creating a feedback loop where it just goes up and up and up as everyone scrambles to buy back from the same limited pool of shares.
 One of the GameStop short sellers is Andrew Left, called   “Wall Street’s Bounty Hunter” by  The New York Times because of his reputation for shorting companies he considers weak and following up by publishing research about why the company is going to fail, or, in some cases,   alleging outright fraud. Yesterday, Left put out a six and a half minute video on YouTube   making his case for why GameStop is doomed. WallStreetBets in turn organized what finance pundit Jim Cramer called “  an ambush,” pumping up up GameStop’s stock in   a coordinated campaign to “squeeze” Left, forcing him to buy tons of stock of his own to “cover” his position and in turn making their shares worth even more. Jim Cramer is   an absolute goon, but if anything fits the bill of “Mad Money” it’s this.
  Left’s research account, Citron, hasn’t posted on Twitter   since yesterday. “Suck it, @CitronResearch,” a GameStop investor relations parody account   tweeted today.
  GameStop’s current stock situation then likely has less to do with its long-term prospects actually looking up than chaotic amatuer day-traders exacerbating existing weirdnesses in the marketplace. Some of those bullish on GameStop’s fortunes may have rightly sniffed out that short sellers were  too negative on the company’s future. As with everything on the internet, what may have started as some people trying to (and succeeding at) making a bunch of quick cash has become much more, including a sort of crusade against Left as well as an unlikely source of GameStop fandom.
 Earlier this month, GameStop announced Ryan Cohen   would be taking a seat on its board. Cohen is formerly the CEO of the pet food website Chewy.com, but he’s already become a golden boy meme in the world of GameStop stocks. Search his name on Twitter and you’ll find   people tweeting things like “Papa Cohen will take us all to mars suit up homies it will be a bumpy ride to the [rocket emoji] so u don’t fall off.”
  It’s hard to know how much of this is genuine cult of personality, and how much of it is internet irony, but it speaks to what a bizarre moment GameStop is having right now and the unlikely confluence of events that created it.

陶瓷重量轻且坚硬，但您无法用它们制造喷气发动机，因为它们会像餐盘一样破碎。因此，材料科学家一直试图模仿将强度（抗变形能力）和韧性（抗断裂能力）结合起来的天然材料。特别是，他们研究了被称为珍珠质的多孔但有弹性的材料作为鲍鱼贝壳的衬里。现在，研究人员已经开发出一种在实验室中制造珍珠母状材料的方法。这些新材料的机械性能类似于金属合金，是有史以来最坚硬的陶瓷。这种新方法可以为节能建筑和轻便但有弹性的汽车框架开发陶瓷结构材料。
    珍珠母（Nacre）也被称为珍珠母，将坚硬但又易碎的碳酸钙板块与柔软的蛋白质胶粘在一起，形成坚固的砖瓦结构，比任何一种都要坚硬3000倍。通常，当科学家在实验室中制作复合材料时，所得材料的特性会平均其成分的特性。加利福尼亚大学伯克利分校材料科学与工程系主任罗伯特·里奇（Robert Ritchie）说：“当大自然制造复合材料时，其性能会更好。”那是因为大自然的复合材料具有难以模仿的复杂结构。里奇说：“人们已经尝试过，但无法获得如此精细的结构。”
 多年来，科学家一直在尝试基于坚硬的天然材料（如珍珠母和骨头）设计新材料。加州理工学院的材料科学家朱莉娅·格里尔（Julia Greer）说，伯克利陶瓷“确实表明，从自然界中汲取灵感以合成更好的材料可能会非常成功。”
 为了将陶瓷塑造成珍珠质结构，伯克利的研究人员首先创建了一种将要图案化的材料（在这种情况下为氧化铝）的水悬浮液。然后他们以非常可控的方式将其冷却。 Ritchie解释说：“您可以消除热量。”这导致长而薄的结构，研究人员在加热它们以蒸发水后将其压制成微型的砖状结构。重复此过程后，它会形成一层氧化铝多孔砖的多层多孔结构，这些多孔砖状结构通过圆柱状结构相互连接-与天然珍珠母中的形状相同。然后，为了模仿鲍鱼壳中的蛋白质胶，研究人员用一种聚合物填充了这些空间。本周《科学》杂志在线描述了这一过程。其他小组已经制作了仿生材料薄膜。伯克利集团成功地制造了大件作品。
  不受聚合物的影响，砖像大多数陶瓷一样易碎。但是，聚合物在受压时允许砖状层彼此滑动，从而使材料具有抗断裂性。确实，这种实体结构比实验室中制造的任何陶瓷都要坚硬。普渡大学的材料工程师埃里克·斯塔克（Eric Stach）说：“高韧性和高强度通常在陶瓷中是不相容的”，他没有参与伯克利的研究。但斯塔克说，伯克利生产的陶瓷具有与铝合金一样的强度和韧性，“您可以用它来驾驶飞机”。
 尽管他们警告说类似珍珠母的陶瓷尚处于发展初期，但伯克利研究人员说，这种材料应该使似乎无法实现的陶瓷应用成为可能。 Ritchie说：“您可以使用陶瓷代替钢来制造汽车的框架，并节省燃料。”劳伦斯伯克利实验室（Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory）的材料科学家安东尼·汤姆西亚（Antoni Tomsia）共同领导了这项研究。他说，坚韧的陶瓷是一种很好的绝缘体，可以作为节能建筑的结构元素而发挥双重作用。它们还可以用于轻质防弹背心和军用车辆装甲。
 材料科学家说，这项新工作为硬质仿生材料指明了前进的方向。加州大学圣塔芭芭拉分校的物理学教授保罗·汉斯玛（Paul Hansma）称这项工作“令人惊讶”，并说新型陶瓷的性能“提高了这一重要领域的水平。” 
Ritchie和Tomsia相信他们可以使材料更好。 天然珍珠层的陶瓷结构比伯克利材料的陶瓷结构小一个数量级，并且砖与砂浆的比率更高。 里奇说，该小组正在致力于使陶瓷砖变得更小，更紧密，并减少聚合物含量。 他们还在试验不同的迫击炮。 由于新开发的陶瓷包含粘性聚合物，因此在高温环境（例如发动机内部）中会失效。 因此，伯克利大学的研究人员正在试验可以承受更高温度的金属填料。
note_en:
Ceramics are lightweight and hard, but you can’t make jet engines out of them because they’d shatter like dinner plates. So, materials scientists have been trying to mimic natural materials that combine  strength (a measure of resistance to deformation) with toughness (a measure of resistance to fracture). In particular, they’ve looked to the porous but resilient material called nacre that lines abalone shells. Now researchers have developed a method for manufacturing nacre-like materials in the lab. These new materials have mechanical properties similar to metal alloys and are the toughest ceramics ever made. The new method could lead the way to ceramic structural materials for energy-efficient buildings and lightweight but resilient automobile frames.
    Nacre, also known as mother-of-pearl, combines plates of stong but brittle calcium carbonate with a soft protein glue in a brick-and-mortar structure that’s 3,000 times tougher than either constituent. Ordinarily, when scientists make composites in the lab, the resulting materials’ properties average out those of their constituents. “When nature makes composites, the properties are better,” says  Robert Ritchie, chair of the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at the University of California, Berkeley, who co-led the ceramics research. That’s because nature’s  composites have complex structures that are difficult to mimic. “People have tried, but can’t get that fineness of structure,” says Ritchie.
 For years, scientists have been trying to design new materials based on tough natural materials like nacre and  bone. The Berkeley ceramic “really shows that drawing our inspiration from nature in order to synthesize better materials can be very successful,” says  Julia Greer, a  materials scientist at CalTech.
 To shape their ceramics into nacre-like structures, the Berkeley researchers first create a water suspension of the material to be patterned–in this case, aluminum oxide. Then they chill it in a very controlled way. “You take the heat out at one end,” explains Ritchie. This leads to long, thin structures that the researchers press into microscale, brick-like structures after heating them to evaporate the water. When this process is repeated, it creates a layered, porous structure of aluminum oxide bricks connected to one another by column-like structures–the same shapes found in natural nacre. Then, to mimic the protein glue in the abalone shell, the researchers fill the spaces with a polymer. This process is described online in the journal   Science this week. Other groups have made thin films of biomimetic materials; the Berkeley group has succeeded in making large pieces.
  Uncushioned by the polymer, the bricks would be brittle like most ceramics. But the polymer permits the brick-like layers to slide over one another when stressed, making the material resistant to fractures. Indeed, this brick-and-mortar structure is tougher than any ceramic ever made in the lab. “High toughness and high strength are usually incompatible” in a ceramic, says  Eric Stach, a materials engineer at Purdue University who was not involved with the Berkeley work. But the ceramics created at Berkeley have as much strength and toughness as aluminum alloys, which “you can fly planes with,” says Stach.
 Though they caution that the nacre-like ceramics are in their early stages of development, the Berkeley researchers say the materials should make possible applications of ceramics that have seemed unattainable. “You could use ceramics to make the frame of a car instead of steel, and save fuel,” says Ritchie. Antoni Tomsia, a materials scientist at the Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory who co-led the research, says that tough ceramics, which are good insulators, could do double duty as structural elements in energy-efficient buildings. And they might also be used in lightweight bulletproof vests and vehicle armor for the military.
 The new work, say materials scientists, shows the way forward for tough biomimetic materials.  Paul Hansma, professor of physics at the University of California at Santa Barbara, calls the work “astonishing” and says the performance of the new ceramic “raises the bar in this important field.”
 Ritchie and Tomsia are confident they can make the material even better. Natural nacre has ceramic structures an order of magnitude smaller than those in the Berkeley material, as well as a higher ratio of brick to mortar. Ritchie says the group is working on making the ceramic bricks smaller and closer together, and decreasing the polymer content. They’re also experimenting with different mortars. Because the newly developed ceramic contains a gluey polymer, it would fail in high-temperature environments like the inside of an engine. So the Berkeley researchers are experimenting with metal fillers, which can withstand higher temperatures.

With this week’s desktop browser update ( v1.19),  Brave is the first browser to offer a native IPFS integration, enabling users to seamlessly browse the decentralized Web, and increasing content availability and Internet resilience. IPFS, or InterPlanetary File System, is a peer-to-peer network and protocol designed to make the web faster, safer, and more open. _____________  Brian Bondy, Brave CTO & Co-founder Brian R. Bondy is the co-founder, CTO and lead developer at Brave Software. Other notable projects he’s worked on include Khan Academy, Mozilla and Evernote. He also co-founded VisionWorks Solutions. Brian was a senior Firefox platform engineer at Mozilla, Linux software developer at Army Simulation Centre, device drive developer at ALT Software, and researcher and software developer at Corel Corporation. He was awarded a Microsoft MVP award for Visual C++ July 2010 - July 2011, and is also in the top 0.1% of contributors on StackOverflow.Brian was chosen by Futurpreneur Canada to represent Canada’s entrepreneurs at the upcoming G20 Young Entrepreneurs’ Alliance Summit in Buenos Aires from September 18th – 21st.Brian was also the winner of the  Lone Cactus Last Person Standing virtual ultramarathon, after having completed 31 laps for a total of 208 km (129 miles) almost entirely outdoors in Canada.
 Dietrich Ayala, IPFS Ecosystem Lead Dietrich Ayala is committed to making a web that puts users in control of their experience. He leads ecosystem development for IPFS at Protocol Labs, growing adoption of the protocol through developer experience, browser integrations and strategic collaborations. Before Protocol Labs, he spent over a decade at Mozilla building browsers, shipping a smartphone OS and running programs to scale globally. Ask us anything! _____________  Download Brave, free:  https://brave.com/  IPFS official website:  https://ipfs.io/  Read the official IPFS integration announcement on Brave’s  blog .  Read the  technical blog  from Brave CTO & Co-founder, Brian Bondy.  Watch the  video  on How to Use IPFS With the Brave Browser.  See IPFS’s official announcement on their  blog .  Reporting Issues and Learning More:
 You can read more about the implementation of IPFS in Brave from its spec  here.
  We’d love to hear suggestions on how we can improve IPFS support in Brave. Issues can be posted  here.

shecc是针对32位Arm和RISC-V体系结构从头开始构建的，它是针对C语言子集的自编译器。
  两遍编译：在第一遍，它检查语句的语法并构建符号表，而在第二遍，它实际上将程序语句转换为Arm / RISC-V机器代码。
       stage0：shecc源代码最初是使用生成本机可执行文件的普通编译器进行编译的。生成的编译器可用作跨编译器。
 stage1：生成的二进制文件读取其自身的源代码作为输入，并生成ARMv7-A / RV32IM二进制文件。
 stage2：生成的ARMv7-A / RV32IM二进制文件以其自身的源代码作为输入被调用（通过QEMU或运行在Arm和RISC-V设备上），并生成另一个ARMv7-A / RV32IM二进制文件。
 bootstrap：构建stage1和stage2编译器，并验证它们在字节方向上是相同的。如果是这样，shecc可以编译自己的源代码并产生同一程序的新版本。
  shecc中的代码生成器不依赖外部实用程序。您只需要通用的C编译器，例如gcc和clang。但是，shecc会自行启动，因此需要Arm / RISC-V ISA仿真。在GNU / Linux上为Arm / RISC-V用户仿真安装QEMU： 
仍然可以在macOS或Microsoft Windows上构建shecc。但是，由于缺少qemu-arm，第二阶段自举将失败。
   $ make config ARCH = arm＃目标机器代码切换到Arm $ make config ARCH = riscv＃目标机器代码切换到RISC-V

 int fib（int n）{如果（n == 0）返回0;否则（n == 1）返回1;返回fib（n-1）+ fib（n-2）;}

shecc is built from scratch, targeted at 32-bit Arm and RISC-V architecture, asa self-compiling compiler for a subset of the C language.
  Two-pass compilation: on the first pass it checks the syntax ofstatements and constructs a table of symbols, while on the second passit actually translates program statements into Arm/RISC-V machine code.
       stage0:  shecc source code is initially compiled using an ordinary compilerwhich generates a native executable. The generated compiler can be used as across-compiler.
 stage1: The built binary reads its own source code as input and generates anARMv7-A/RV32IM binary.
 stage2: The generated ARMv7-A/RV32IM binary is invoked (via QEMU or running onArm and RISC-V devices) with its own source code as input and generates anotherARMv7-A/RV32IM binary.
 bootstrap: Build the  stage1 and  stage2 compilers, and verify that they arebyte-wise identical. If so,  shecc can compile its own source code and producenew versions of that same program.
  Code generator in  shecc does not rely on external utilities. You only needordinary C compilers such as  gcc and  clang. However,  shecc would bootstrapitself, and Arm/RISC-V ISA emulation is required. Install QEMU for Arm/RISC-V useremulation on GNU/Linux:
  It is still possible to build  shecc on macOS or Microsoft Windows. However,the second stage bootstrapping would fail due to  qemu-arm absence.
   $ make config ARCH=arm# Target machine code switch to Arm$ make config ARCH=riscv# Target machine code switch to RISC-V

  Once the option  --dump-ir is passed to  shecc, the intermediate representation (IR)will be generated. Take the file  tests/fib.c for example. It consists of a recursiveFibonacci sequence function.
 int  fib( int n){  if (n ==  0)  return  0;  else  if (n ==  1)  return  1;  return  fib(n -  1) +  fib(n -  2);}

Compare x0 with x1  if false then goto  1641  If x0 != x1 ,  then jump to label  1641  return  0 ; x0 := 0 Set x0 to zero. x0 is the return value.  return (from fib) Jump to function exit   1641 :  else if (n ==  1 ) x0 = &n Get address of variable n  x0 =  * x0 ( 4 ) Read value from address  into  x0 ,  length =  4  ( int )  x1 :=  1  Set x1 to  1  x0 == x1 ? Compare x0 with x1  if true then goto  1649  If x0 != x1 ,  then jump to label  1649  return  1 ; x0 := 1 Set x0 to 1. x0 is the return value.  return (from fib) Jump to function exit   1649 :  return x0 = &n Get address of variable n  fib(n  -   1 ) x0 =  * x0 ( 4 ) Read value from address  into  x0 ,  length =  4  ( int )  x1 :=  1  Set x1 to  1  x0  - = x1 Subtract x1 from x0 i.e. (n  -   1 )   +  x0 := fib() @  1631   Call  function fib()  into  x0   push  x0 Store the result on stack  fib(n  -   2 ) ; x0 = &n Get address of variable n  x0 =  * x0 ( 4 ) Read value from address  into  x0 ,  length =  4  ( int )  x1 :=  2  Set x1 to  2  x0  - = x1 Subtract x1 from x0 i.e. (n  -   2 )  x1 := fib() @  1631   Call  function fib()  into  x1   pop  x0 Retrieve the result off stack  into  x0  x0  + = x1  Add  x1 to x0 i.e. the result of fib(n - 1 )  +  fib(n - 2 )  return (from fib) Jump to function exit  } Restore the previous stack frame  exit fib
  The unary  * operator is not supported, which makes it necessary to use  [0] syntax.Consider  int x = 5; int *ptr = &x; and it is forbidden to use  *ptr. However, it is valid touse  ptr[0], which behaves the same of  *ptr.

你好朋友！当前对“现实生活”的强制要求使我目前的目标是，除了周五更新外，每周发布一次。
 最后，这是一个名为“俱乐部会所的秘密历史”的新系列的第1部分。标题可能看起来有点像是点击诱饵（可能有点正确），但它是从同伴作品到《双峰》的第3季，也是对俱乐部历史如何是口述历史的认可。
 这样的话，就故事的传承本质而言，本系列文章并非纯粹是客观的。用安德鲁·罗宾逊（Andrew Robinson）的话来形容，他在《星际迷航：深空9》中扮演了神秘的加拉克，这是我们人类所能做的最好的事情，它对俱乐部会所发生的事件的偏见，偏斜的回忆。
 以前，我曾尝试通过书面形式维护Chatham House规则，并在此处大体遵循这些规则。这不是在各种俱乐部会所发生的事件中使用真实姓名的暴露，而是通过社区建设和解散的迷人旅程重新讲述事件。我希望本系列会有趣且有趣，但对互联网VIP俱乐部以外的其他人也很有用。
   对于不知道的人，Clubhouse是一个基于音频的社交网络，它是播客和派对节目的一部分。用户加入不同的房间，然后加入对话或收听。似乎不太可能，这种基于音频的社交网络的相对简单的实现方式实现了无与伦比的“ it-factor”，如果它受到与安全性和表达自由有关的所有难题的困扰，而这些难题几乎涉及到每个人。其他社交媒体平台。
 创始人一直坚持要尽快向公众开放Clubhouse，但不能否认平台的当前仅受邀状态已成为其炒作因素。自今年3月左右以来，它一直处于活动状态，而自9月初以来，我一直在使用它，当时用户数接近7,000。在Andreesen-Horowitz（也称为a16z）的支持下，Clubhouse的增长轨迹非常有利。
 如果说模仿是最真诚的奉承形式，那么Clubhouse就会喜出望外。除了Telegram添加了类似功能外，Twitter还宣布了其名为“音频空间”的嵌入式音频聊天的实现。诸如Sonar之类的其他平台则尝试区别对待而不是复制Clubhouse的体验，而不再将音频聊天视为电话会议，而将其视为空间音频视频游戏。 
最终，Clubhouse是一个让我快速坠入爱河的平台。当我加入时，我的俱乐部会所经历使人们进入了充满激情的讨论的美妙门户，一种热情而包容的文化，而且这个空间甚至连我的社交焦虑都无法阻止我享受。
 现在，Clubhouse混血儿。在9月份，我会用戴尔·库珀（Dale Cooper）描述双峰的方式描述它：奇妙而奇怪。现在？我的一位朋友描述得最好：有时候很有趣。
   由于会所房间的短暂性质，会所历史是“秘密的”。没有自动抄录，最后一个人离开后，房间消失了。更重要的是，未经允许的手动抄写（尽管最近有一些文章同时公开了真实姓名和对话内容）违反了《 Clubhouse社区准则》。因此，人们自然会问整个“秘密历史”系列的一个问题：为什么？
 在快速扩展的这段期间里，我很幸运能够进入Clubhouse（也许几个月前我没找到办法，但仍然有些失望）。发生的事件以及我从中获得的收获都对信任与安全，社区建设，人际互动等产生了影响。自上次在Clubhouse上沉思以来，我一直想写更多的东西，但是时间不对。
 由于过去几周的事件，现在感觉到了合适的时机。美国有一位新总统，其旧任总统已经接受了亚历克斯·琼斯的退台待遇。唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）的推文引发的骚乱导致5人死亡，该国政府机构遭到破坏，立法者处于封锁状态。
 播客在某些情况下被称为“漏洞”，通过该漏洞，那些现在被其他平台禁止的播客仍然能够发出信息。毫无疑问，这场战斗将很快出现在像Clubhouse这样的音频社交媒体上。
 由于新兴的音频社交生态系统，也感觉到了合适的时机。 Clubhouse是最著名的，但是Sonar和Twitter自己的“ Spaces”等其他平台也试图抢占市场份额。 “高级”平台的历史与其竞争对手息息相关，因为他们试图复制Clubhouse的成功并利用其失败。 
在大多数情况下，这似乎是正确的时机，因为对我而言，Clubhouse的魔力已经结束。如果有的话，Clubhouse现在是整个社会的加强，这对LGBTQ +人士而言既不是欢迎也不是友好的地方。
  俱乐部会所的开发始于2019年，并借鉴了创始人的先前产品（保罗·戴维森和罗汉·塞思）的“ Talk Show”。 Clubhouse在App Store上的描述是“嵌入式音频聊天”，基本上可以用于临时播客。有一个演讲者的舞台，一个听众的观众。
 Clubhouse平台从一个无标题的“房间”开始，扩展到多个无标题的房间，到今天为止，它已经包含了上千个并发标题房间。会所历史中提到了几个不同的文化时期，“夏季会所”就是其中之一。我不是去参加夏季俱乐部会所的，但在我们不确定人类是否会从全球大流行中脱颖而出之际，这是崇高的敬意。那时，人口仍然非常集中在硅谷的创始社区，风险投资家和各色名人之间。约翰·梅耶（John Mayer）的露面成为一个模因，并引发了名人围攻现象。事实证明，一个有成就的音乐家真的想谈论政治和迷幻主义者。
 在早期，我第一次真正听到并意识到了该平台，主要是在《纽约时报》的技术/文化记者与SV / VC社区的知名成员之间广为宣传的行列中。它读起来就像许多其他文化大战一样，所以我当时并没有考虑太多。报道显示，Clubhouse似乎是精英人士将技术会议和运动场结合在一起的方式，这激发了FOMO的灵感，但对我而言，除了荣耀的Discord之外，其他一切并没有给我带来什么惊喜。
 后来，我的一个Twitter朋友发布了有关使用Clubhouse的简短YouTube视频。再次，这似乎只是一次音频聊天，尽管与一些有趣的人在一起（大约在这个时候，奥普拉在平台上开始声名狼藉）。纽约时报有更多关于该平台骚扰的报道，并且一些俱乐部会谈不时地出现在我的Twitter时间轴上。
 8月下旬的某个晚上，这一切都达到了顶峰，FOMO让我变得更好。我用谷歌搜索了5分钟（之前的尝试大约是30秒），最终找到了一份ScreenRant文章，其中列出了Clubhouse网站（Clubhouse的确不能被指控过多投放广告）。于是我四处逛逛，发现那里有一个加入候补名单的地方。我在Twitter上对此感到讨厌，并从平台上的一个朋友那里收到了一份DM，他说他们将支持我的应用程序（Clubhouse网站上有一个小文本框，提及您可能已经在平台上认识的人）。
 我单击了提交，却忘记了它。第二天，从一条相当残酷的根管回家后，我收到一个陌生号码的奇怪文字，说我有邀请。我当时非常震惊，但是由于我当时只有一部Android手机，因此需要等待几天（并且不会因为亲自在某处购买新手机而冒着社交媒体平台的健康风险）。因此，我订购了一部iPhone并等待其到货。 
终于收到iPhone之后，我第一次打开了Clubhouse。 UI绝对让我不知所措，它似乎是一个非常简单的音频聊天应用程序。我添加了在平台上找到的几个我认识的朋友，他们搜寻了平台上还有哪些其他知名人士，然后开始听一些房间。那时，任何一次都只有几个房间打开，有时根本没有房间打开。
 当时我还不知道，但是Clubhouse的早期社区已经建立了一个非常热情和包容的环境。那些直接被“邀请”的人通常是由邀请者“加入”的，也就是说，他们被简要介绍了如何使用该平台以及一些文化规范。当时，Clubhouse的一个非常有趣的功能是与创始人以及当周加入平台的其他人进行了个性化的交流。我非常喜欢这次会议，有机会与Paul聊聊自己的在线历史，对Clubhouse的积极经历以及我希望它会（也不会）成为现实。 Rohan也出席了会议（但口语一般都比较柔和，Paul具有旺盛的精力和谈话风格），我非常感谢有机会直接与创始团队交谈。
 即使是早期的Clubhouse，也出现了一些确实很棒的文化规范（与最近发表的有关Clubhouse的一些不准确的文章相反，这些规范早于2020年秋天新成员迅速涌入之前）。由于Clubhouse不具有内部短信功能，因此许多俱乐部使用个人资料图片进行视觉交流。人们还使用这些图片更改来玩游戏，并且一群早期用户实际上设法打破了Clubhouse的后端，以至于Rohan毫无提示地进入了房间进行调查。对于这个问题是否促使平台处理图片的方式发生了改变，我还不是100％清楚，但是＆＃39; PTR'或Pull-To-Refresh的规范是新人们首先要做的事情之一讲了。本质上，在俱乐部会所房间中“拉”会刷新所有图片，如果人们更改图片并希望其他人看到它，人们会说“ PTR”。
 PTR是技术性更强的规范之一，与其他已发展的文化规范相比，它实际上是次要的。假设有最好的意图，“安静地离开”以免打断谈话的交流，当我加入时，为他人腾出空间都是相当普遍的。好奇，接受和热情的社会契约似乎也得到了大多数人的同意。这与Twitter形成了鲜明的对比，并且是使我真正着迷于平台的一件事。最重要的是，我希望至少有一份有关这种简单而卓越的文化框架的书面记录，以使Clubhouse得以迅速发展。正是这种框架导致了引人注目的引人注目：一个有言语障碍（即我）的神经多样的人找到了社交互动的最佳去处。
 通常，我更喜欢文本媒体，例如Substack用于长篇文章，或者Twitter用于短形式戏ter。但是，这次我真的很喜欢Clubhouse，绝对比Twitter更喜欢。俱乐部会所的交流比文字交流更令人满意，但不像与某人进行“面对面”互动那样费劲，因为没有肢体语言提示需要注意，也没有眼神交流等等。最重要的是，我从来没有因为歧视的交流方式而真正感到歧视。在我的低谷时期，Twitter会使我的情绪变得更糟而不是变得更好，而早期的Clubhouse则相反。保罗曾在各种场合说过，他希望人们在使用该平台后感觉更好，而我所做的程度使我合理地表示我几次说过的话，我将永远不会注销（并可能困扰该平台。一个鬼）。这当然不再正确，主要是由于平台上存在猖the的拖钓和其他信任与安全问题。俱乐部会所杰出的原因之一是一个很简单的原因：完全没有拖钓。
 此时，Clubhouse的另一个特征使我成为Clubhouse的传播者，这是几乎不存在的几乎所有其他社交媒体平台都无法表达的书面语音代码。似乎没有话题超出范围，似乎还可以进行实际的讨论，而不仅仅是人们像在Twitter上大喊大叫。
 特别是，我能够与某人就反激进主义的棘手方面进行非常有趣的讨论。它不仅令人耳目一新，而且是其他所有社交媒体平台上存在的社交功能障碍的一片绿洲。 
还吸引了一些关注者，一位着名的科技/游戏企业家定期举办房间，这些房间的特征是“另类”，更多是播客，更少的对话，并且在多个层​​面上都很有趣。还有一种“夜店后黑夜”现象，导致平台上的夜间交谈本质上更加成人化，而在这一天，俱乐部会所往往很讲TED。
 早期的Clubhouse，而与Twitter相比，乌托邦确实存在其问题。在“硅谷女性”演讲中，一些演讲者被听众吓倒了，以至于听众中的某人“平呼”（邀请）一些被卷入法律斗争的男性科技高管/创始人。在平台上讨论的政治方面也存在明显的紧张局势，可以说，此时平台人群倾向于向左倾斜。最终，问题（尽管与今天相比较小）和不断的新闻报道导致Clubhouse进行了重大的平台更改。
   Clubhouse提出了录制所有音频对话（临时）的想法，因此信任和安全性值得一提，而不是试图追踪房间里的人，这是令人反感的。正是这一举动促使我创建了我的第一个房间，标题为“会所会录制音频导致自上而下审核？”。在这里，我认识了许多目前的Clubhouse朋友，这真是一次很棒的经历，讨论的范围非常广泛，持续了数小时。种族这一敏感话题以一种意想不到的方式出现，这确实使我对社交媒体治理有了不同的看法。
 当我说Twitter过去很好时，我真的希望Clubhouse不会像平台那样发展，有人提出了Black Planet用户迁移到Twitter时发生的事情：关于平台的变化以及如何变化有类似的评论在平台上谈论文化变革确实可以是谈论种族的一种编码方式（许多雇主使用“文化契合度”作为解决种族歧视的一种方式）。我根本不了解这种迁移（我的评论主要针对的是如今遍布Twitter的“ Bluecheck”文化）。在这些早期的会议中，您真正学习到一些东西的能力确实给我留下了深刻的印象。
 我继续创建并加入更多房间，并真正开始与平台上的许多房间建立联系。自私的是，我会从Clubhouse带走的最伟大的事情之一就是得到一些精英的认可。在这些异常不确定的时期，让一些最有成就的女性伸出援手，无疑是一个信心的提升。在最近的一次电话会议中，我提到这是“看起来很小”的事情，其他人实际上讨论了这笔交易有多大。我也竭尽全力欢迎其他人，非常喜欢谈论该平台，并且是创始人每周举办的“市政厅”的定期撰稿人。我经常把俱乐部会所比作《燃烧的人》，这是一件了不起的事情（部分原因是燃烧的人比例很高），这是我无法得到的神奇的偶然体验。
 俱乐部会所的组织通过两种方法进行，即“日历”和“俱乐部”。日历是任何人都可以添加的事件的时间表，并且俱乐部的​​功能类似于Facebook群组。在此期间，日历很小，俱乐部尚无法搜索。一段时间以来，我经营一家俱乐部，以新颖有趣的方式使用该平台，效果相当不错。从我的早期经验来看，这两种机制都将在许多事件中起关键作用。
 对于早期社区而言，它最好的特征是“孩子们通常不会在午餐时坐在同一张桌子旁互动”的经历，这的确是事实。娱乐界，科技界和其他地方的人们确实确实在交流思想和相处。这确实是值得一看的东西。我将始终为加入的人员的才干而感到惊讶，以及着名的人员如何成为像我这样的Clubhouse的成员，并以好奇心和学习意愿接近平台的。我曾经在俱乐部俱乐部中遇到的一种超现实的经历是，我了解《我愿意》对表达自由的态度与我大致相同。 
在这个阶段加入社区的许多人后来都会告诉我，他们知道这种情况不会持续下去。我认为我的大脑中的一小部分也知道这一点，但是我仍然乐观地认为，也许，如果我继续提出正确的政策建议，这个令人耳目一新的平台将是一个机会。我当然是错的，事情最终进展得和其他地方一样。
 但是，它以一种奇怪的方式实现了。在影响其他平台（如Twitter）治理的活动中，我扮演了背景角色，没有足够的信心去表达我在Clubhouse上发表的许多意见。尽管我目前对平台有种感觉，但我还是非常感激能成为早期用户，无论是我的经历还是与我建立的关系。并非所有人都幸存下来，但其中一些人幸免于难，这就是在脱节的时代中发生了什么。会所确实会滋生一些虚假的亲密关系，但可能不超过夏季节日之类的临时社区。对于那些最终破裂的联系，我仍然感激它们的存在，以及它们给我留下深刻印象的临时社区的力量。
 下次：肖恩·斯派塞（Sean Spicer）和安东尼·斯卡拉穆奇（Anthony Scaramucci）是如何导致会所Twitterati出埃及的？
 因此，我们结束了Clubhouse历史的第一部分。这部分时间跨度从9月初到10月中旬左右，这是我在俱乐部历史上最喜欢的时间，其中包含一些我将永远珍惜的回忆。第2部分将介绍Clubhouse Overton窗口迅速缩小的开始，以及许多早期用户的外流。
 是什么原因引起的呢？一个重要的周末始于肖恩·斯派塞（Sean Spicer）和安东尼·斯卡塔姆奇（Anthony Scaramucci）的邀请。
note_en:
Hello friends! The current imposition of Real Life has necessitated I currently aim for a post frequency of once a week in addition to Friday updates.
 At long last here is part 1 of a new series entitled ‘The Secret History of Clubhouse’. The title might seem like a bit of clickbait (this might be a little bit true), but it’s taken both from the companion piece to season 3 of  Twin Peaks, and as an acknowledgement of how Clubhouse history is oral history.
 As such, this series will not be purely objective, by the very nature of how stories are handed down. To paraphrase Andrew Robinson, who portrayed the enigmatic Garak on  Star Trek : Deep Space 9: This biased, skewed recollection of events on Clubhouse is the best we humans can do.
 Previously, I’ve tried to maintain Chatham House rules in writing about the platform, and will mostly adhere to them here. This is not some expose of putting real names to various Clubhouse happenings, but a re-telling of events through a fascinating journey in community building and dissolution. I’m hopeful this series will be entertaining and intriguing, but also useful to others outside of the internet’s VIP club.
   For the unaware, Clubhouse is an audio-based social network which is part podcast and party line. Users join different rooms, and either join the conversations or listen. Unlikely as it may seem, this relatively simple implementation of an audio-based social network is what has achieved an unparalleled ‘it-factor’, if one that has been plagued by the same conundrums about safety and freedom of expression that concern just about every other social media platform.
 While the founders have been adamant they want to open Clubhouse to the public as soon as possible, it’s impossible to deny that the current invite-only state of the platform has added to its hype factor. It has been active since around March of this year, and I’ve been on it since early September when the user count was closer to 7,000. Backed by Andreesen-Horowitz (also known as a16z), the growth trajectory for Clubhouse is very favourable.
 If imitation is the sincerest form of flattery, Clubhouse is beaming. Twitter has announced their implementation of drop-in audio chat, called ‘Audio Spaces’, in addition to Telegram adding a similar feature. Other platforms such as Sonar, which treats audio chat less like a conference call and more like a spatial audio video game, are attempting to differentiate rather than replicate the Clubhouse experience.
 Ultimately, Clubhouse is a platform that I rapidly fell both in and out of love with. When I joined, my Clubhouse experience was of some wonderful kind of portal to fulsome discussion, a welcoming and inclusive culture, and a space that not even my social anxiety could prevent me from enjoying.
 Now, Clubhouse is a mixed bag. In September, I would have described it in the same way Dale Cooper describes Twin Peaks : Wonderful and strange. Now? One of my friends described it best : It’s sometimes fun.
   Clubhouse history is ‘secret’, because of the ephemeral nature of Clubhouse rooms. There is no automated transcription, and the rooms disappear after the last person leaves. More to the point, manual transcription (despite some recent articles both disclosing real names and conversation content) without permission is against the Clubhouse Community Guidelines. So, one might naturally ask a question of this entire ‘Secret History’ series : Why?
 I feel very fortunate to have been on Clubhouse (and maybe still a little disappointed I didn’t find a way in a few months prior) during this period of rapid scaling. The events that transpired, and what I took away from them have implications for Trust and Safety, community building, human interaction, and more. I’ve always wanted to write more since my last piece musing on Clubhouse, but the time didn’t feel right.
 It now feels like the right time, due to the events of the past few weeks. The USA has a new President, and its old one has been given the Alex Jones de-platforming treatment. A riot incited by Donald Trump’s tweets saw 5 dead, the nation’s houses of government breached and lawmakers in lockdown as the situation unfolded.
 Podcasts are referred to by some as a  ‘loophole’ by which those now banned from other platforms are still able to get their message out. This battle will undoubtedly come to audio social media like Clubhouse in short order.
 It also feels like the right time due to the emerging audio social ecosystem. Clubhouse is the most well known, but other platforms like Sonar and Twitter’s own ‘Spaces’ seek to seize their share of the market as well. The history of the ‘prime’ platform is very relevant to their competitors, as they seek to replicate Clubhouse’s successes and capitalize on its failures.
 Mostly it seems like the right time because for me, the magic of Clubhouse is over. If anything, Clubhouse is now a reinforcement of wider society, which is to say neither a welcoming nor friendly place for LGBTQ+ folks.
  Clubhouse development began in 2019, and borrowed from a previous product of the founders (Paul Davison and Rohan Seth) called ‘Talk Show’. The App Store description of Clubhouse is ‘drop-in audio chat’, which basically works out to an ephemeral podcast. There’s a stage for speakers, and an audience for listeners.
 The Clubhouse platform began with one untitled ‘room’, which expanded to multiple untitled rooms, and as of today consists of over a thousand concurrent titled rooms. There are several distinct cultural time periods that are referred to in Clubhouse history, ‘Summer Clubhouse’ is one of them. I was not present for summer Clubhouse, but it’s spoken about with reverence as a time of genuine connection and relationship building during the times where we weren’t sure whether humanity was going to come out of a global pandemic. At that time, the population was very much still concentrated around the founding community of Silicon Valley, venture capitalists, and assorted celebrities. An appearance by John Mayer became a meme, and the phenomenon of celebrity mobbing was born. An accomplished musician, in turned out, just really wanted to talk about politics and psychedelics.
 It was during the early times that I first really heard and became aware of the platform, mostly over the well publicized row between a New York Times tech / culture reporter and a prominent member of the SV / VC community. It read like many other culture war happenings, so I didn’t think too much of it or the platform at the time. The coverage read as though Clubhouse was some sort of combination of tech conference and playground for the elite, something FOMO-inspiring but that didn’t really strike me as anything but a glorified Discord.
 Later, a Twitter friend of mine posted a brief YouTube video of using Clubhouse. It, again, seemed like just another audio chat, albeit with some interesting people (it was around this time that Oprah being on the platform started to achieve notoriety). There was more reporting from the NYT on harassment on the platform, and some Clubhouse chatter made its way into my Twitter timeline from time to time.
 This all came to a head one evening in late August, where the FOMO got the better of me. I googled for a solid 5 minutes (up from previous attempts of around 30 seconds), and eventually found a ScreenRant article that actually listed the  Clubhouse website (Clubhouse can really not ever be accused of excessive advertising). So I poked around, and found there was a place for joining the waitlist. I was obnoxious on Twitter about it, and received a DM from a friend on the platform who said they would support my application (there was a small textbox on the Clubhouse website to mention who you might already know on the platform).
 I clicked submit and forgot about it. The next day, after walking home from a fairly brutal root canal, I got an odd text from an unknown number saying I had an invite. I was pretty shocked, but needed to wait for a few days as I only had an android phone at the time (and wasn’t about to risk my health for a social media platform by buying a new phone in-person somewhere). So I ordered an iPhone and waited for it to arrive.
   After I finally received the iPhone, I opened Clubhouse for the first time. I was definitely underwhelmed by the UI, it seemed like a very simplistic audio chat application. I added the few people I found on the platform that I knew as friends, searched to see which other notable people were on the platform, and started listening to a few rooms. Back then, there were only a few rooms open at any one time, and at times there were no rooms at all open.
 I didn’t know it at the time, but the early community on Clubhouse had built a very welcoming and inclusive environment. Those who were directly ‘invited’ were usually ‘onboarded’ by their inviter, which is to say they were given a brief rundown of how to use the platform and some cultural norms. A very interesting feature of Clubhouse at the time was a personalized session with the founders with others who had joined the platform that week. I really enjoyed my session, getting a chance to chat with Paul about my history of being Very Online, my positive experience with Clubhouse and what I hoped it would (and would not) become. Rohan was also present (but is generally more soft-spoken, Paul has an exuberant energy and conversational style) and I really appreciated the opportunity that was given to talk to the founding team directly.
 Even with early Clubhouse, some really wonderful cultural norms had emerged (and contrary to some of the inaccurate articles published about Clubhouse recently, these norms pre-date the rapid influx of new members in the fall of 2020). Since Clubhouse has no internal text messaging function, many use their profile picture to communicate visually. People also use these picture changes to play games, and a group of early users actually managed to break the Clubhouse back-end to a point that Rohan entered the room unprompted to investigate. I’m not 100% clear on whether this prompted a change in the way pictures were handled by the platform, but the norm of 'PTR’, or Pull-To-Refresh is one of the first things that new people are told about. Essentially, ‘pulling’ on a Clubhouse room will refresh all the pictures, and people will say ‘PTR’ if they change their picture and want others to see it.
 PTR is one of the more technical norms and is really fairly minor compared to the other cultural norms that evolved. Assuming best intentions, ‘leaving quietly’ so as to not break the flow of conversation, and making space for others were all quite prevalent when I joined. There also seemed to be a mostly agreed to social contract of curiosity, acceptance, and welcoming. It was a stark contrast to Twitter, and was one of the things that really hooked me on the platform. More than anything else, I want at least some written record of this simplistic yet remarkable cultural framework to survive Clubhouse’s rapid expansion. It’s this framework that resulted in something quote remarkable : A neurodiverse person with a speech impediment (that’s me) having found a sweet spot for social interaction.
 Generally I prefer a text medium such as Substack for long-form posts, or Twitter for short-form banter. And yet, I really enjoyed Clubhouse at this time, definitely more-so than Twitter. Clubhouse communication was more fulfilling than text, but not as taxing as interacting ‘in-person’ with someone since there were no body language cues to watch for, no eye contact, and so on. On top of that, I never really felt discriminated against due to having an odd manner of communication, which was a nice change. During my low periods, Twitter tends to make my mood worse and not better, whereas early Clubhouse had the opposite effect. Paul has said on various occasions that he wants people to feel better after using the platform, and the degree to which I did made me to legitimately mean what I said a few times, that I would never log off (and possibly haunt the platform as a ghost). This is certainly no longer true, mostly due to the rampant trolling and other trust and safety issues that exist on the platform. One of the reasons Clubhouse was remarkable is a rather simple one : The complete absence of trolling.
 The other feature of Clubhouse at this time that led to me being a Clubhouse evangelist was how the unwritten speech codes that characterize just about every other social media platform seemed to not exist. There did not seem to be topics that were off-limits, and it also seemed like actual discourse was possible, rather than just people yelling politics at each other as they do on Twitter.
 In particular, I was able to have a very fulsome discussion with someone about the thornier aspects of trans activism. It was more than refreshing, it was an oasis from the dreariness of the social dysfunction that exists on every other social media platform.
 Certain followings had also developed, a notable tech/gaming entrepreneur regularly hosted rooms which were characterized by many as ‘alt-right’, and was more of a podcast and less of a conversation, and very entertaining on multiple levels. There was also a ‘Clubhouse after-dark’ phenomena which led to evening conversations on the platform being more adult in nature, during the day Clubhouse tended to be very TED-talky.
 Early Clubhouse, while utopia in comparison to Twitter did have its problems. During a ‘Women in Silicon Valley’ talk, a few of the speakers were intimidated into leaving early by someone in the audience ‘pinging’ (inviting) some male tech executives / founders they were embroiled in a legal battle with. There was also obvious tension in politics discussed on-platform, it’s fair to say that at this point the platform population tended to lean left. Eventually, the issues (although minor in comparison to today) and continual press coverage led to Clubhouse making a major platform change.
   Clubhouse floated the idea of recording all audio conversations (temporarily) so Trust and Safety would have something to refer to rather than having to try and track down people who were in the room was something objectionable was said. It was this move that prompted me to create my first room, entitled something like ‘Will Clubhouse recording audio lead to Top-Down Moderation?’. It was a really great experience where I met many of my current Clubhouse friends, a discussion that was really wide-ranging and went on for hours. The sensitive topic of race came up in an unexpected way that really made me consider a different perspective on social media governance.
 When I said that Twitter used to be good and I really hoped Clubhouse wouldn’t progress in the same way that platform had, someone brought up what happened when Black Planet users migrated to Twitter : There were similar comments about the platform changing, and how talking about culture change on platform can really be a coded way of talking about race (many employers use ‘Culture Fit’ as a way to get around racial discrimination). I wasn’t aware of this migration at all (my comments were mainly directed at the ‘Bluecheck’ culture that now pervades much of Twitter). It really impressed upon me the power of Clubhouse in these early days for discussions where you actually learn something.
 I went on to both create and join more rooms, and really started to connect with many on the platform. Selfishly, one of the greatest things I’ll take away from Clubhouse is validation by some of the elite. Having some of the most accomplished women reach out for a Zoom was quite a confidence boost in these extraordinarily uncertain times. On a recent zoom call, I mentioned this as something that ‘seemed small’, and just how big of a deal it actually was was discussed by others. I also did my best to welcome others, really enjoyed talking about the platform, and was a regular contributor to weekly ‘Town Halls’ hosted by the founders. Clubhouse was really a remarkable thing that  I often likened to Burning Man (in part due to the high percentage of the population being Burners), a magical experience of serendipity that I just couldn’t get enough of.
 Organization on Clubhouse happens through two methods, the ‘Calendar’ and ‘Clubs’. The Calendar is a schedule of events that anyone can add to, and Clubs function in a similar fashion to Facebook groups. During this time the Calendar was rather small, and Clubs were not yet searchable. For a time, I ran a club on using the platform in new and interesting ways that did fairly well. Both of these mechanisms would play a key role in many of the events that were to follow from my early experiences.
 When it comes to the early community, it is probably best characterized as an experience of ‘kids who wouldn’t normally sit together at the same tables at lunch interacting’ and it was really true. People from the entertainment industry, the tech industry, and other places really did seem to be sharing ideas and getting along. It was truly something to behold. I would consistently be amazed by the caliber of people that joined, and how the notable people were just members of Clubhouse such as myself, and approached the platform with curiosity and a willingness to learn. One of the more surreal Clubhouse experiences I had was learning that Will I Am has much of the same attitude as I towards freedom of expression.
 Many who were part of the community at this stage would later tell me that they knew it wouldn’t last. I think a small part of my brain knew this too, but I remained optimistic that maybe, just maybe, if I continued to lay down the right policy advice this amazingly refreshing platform stood a chance. I was wrong of course, and things eventually proceeded much as they have elsewhere.
 It was, however, fulfilling in a strange way. During the events that shaped the governance of other platforms such as Twitter, I played a background role, not having been confident enough to voice many of the opinions that I did on Clubhouse. Despite my current feelings towards the platform, I’m very grateful for being an early user, both for the experiences that I had and the relationships that I developed. Not all of them have survived, but some of them have, and that’s saying something in a era of disconnection. Clubhouse does foster a bit of false intimacy, but probably no more than temporary communities such as summer festivals do. For those connections that eventually fell apart, I’m still grateful for what they were, and for the power of temporary community that they impressed upon me.
 Next Time : How Did Sean Spicer and Anthony Scaramucci Cause The Clubhouse Twitterati Exodus?
 Thus we end the first part of Clubhouse’s history. This part, which spans early September to about the middle of October, was my favourite time in Clubhouse history, and contains some memories I will always cherish. Part 2 will cover the beginning of a rapid narrowing of the Clubhouse Overton window, and the exodus of many early users.
 What could cause such a thing? A fateful weekend that began with the invitation of Sean Spicer and Anthony Scaramucci.
title: 会所，第1部分–起点

note:

note_en:
A home security technician admitted Thursday that he secretly accessed the cameras of more than 200 customers, particularly attractive women, to spy on while they undressed, slept, or had sex, federal prosecutors said.
 Telesforo Aviles, a 35-year-old former employee for the security company ADT, admitted he secretly accessed the customers' accounts more than 9,600 times over more than four years, according to a guilty plea submitted in court.
 "This defendant, entrusted with safeguarding customers' homes, instead intruded on their most intimate moments," acting US Attorney Prerak Shah said in a  statement. "We are glad to hold him accountable for this disgusting betrayal of trust."
 While working for ADT, a home security company that provides alarms, cameras, and locks, Aviles took note of customer homes where attractive women lived, prosecutors said, then repeatedly gained access to their video feeds for sexual gratification.
  The technician violated the company's policies by surreptitiously adding his personal email address to accounts on ADT Pulse, an app that allows customers to check remotely on their homes' security cameras.
 At times, Aviles claimed he needed to temporarily add himself to their accounts to test the security system, prosecutors said. In others, he simply added his email to the account without notifying customers.
 The federal charges against Aviles were filed against him in October 2020, five months after multiple ADT customers filed lawsuits against the company in connection to the security breach.
 ADT, which is based in Florida, is currently facing three federal lawsuits related to the incident. The breach affected 220 customers who lived in Texas.
 According to the lawsuits, ADT began to notify customers about the breach back in April 2020, telling them that one of their employees had accessed customer accounts for about four and a half years.
 In court records, one defendant claims Aviles was found to have accessed the home security cameras of her parent's home at least 73 times between 2017 and 2020, including her bedroom while she was still a teenager.
 In the lawsuit, the woman said ADT had "failed to implement adequate procedures that would prevent non-household members from adding non-household email addresses" to the mobile app.
 Another lawsuit alleges the company had "failed to monitor consumers' accounts and promptly alert them anytime a new email was added to their accounts."
 The lawsuit also claims the flagrant security breach was discovered not by the company, but "by luck and happenstance."
 "A customer, reporting a technical issue, inadvertently revealed the unwanted third-party access," the lawsuit claims. "But for that event, ADT would be unaware of this invasive conduct."
 In a statement to BuzzFeed News, an ADT spokesperson said the company self-reported the incident in April 2020  on its website.
 "After we learned of the unauthorized access, we immediately took preventative steps to ensure this can never happen again, and we personally contacted each of our 220 customers who were impacted by this incident," the statement read.
 In the three lawsuits, the customers also alleged ADT tried to obtain confidentiality agreements when notifying them of the security breach. In one instance, a customer said she was offered $2,500, as well as credit for monitoring services and upgraded equipment.
  The spokesperson for ADT said the company apologized to the people who were affected and addressed the matter differently with each customer.
 "In speaking to our customers and apologizing for what happened, it's clear that the employee's abuse of access impacted each customer differently," the spokesperson said. "Therefore, we took steps to address their concerns individually."
 According to court records, ADT has moved to dismiss the suits, claiming that, per their contracts, customers are required to solve the dispute privately in arbitration.
  ADT in a statement said the company is continuing to respond to the lawsuits and has resolved the concerns of most of the 220 impacted customers, including those who have retained attorneys to address the issue.
 "We remain committed in our attempts to resolve all affected customer concerns," the statement read.
 Meanwhile, Aviles could face up to five years in prison for pleading guilty to computer fraud, prosecutors said.
 He has been allowed to remain out of custody until his sentencing — but in the meantime, he is prohibited from working in a job that gives him access to video security systems.

COVID-19疫苗的推出终于来了。他们希望畜群免疫即将到来，这种免疫是一种防止传染病的方法，一旦有足够比例的人口进行了疫苗接种或感染，这种传染病就不会发生。但是，即使首批获得美国食品药品监督管理局紧急使用授权的疫苗在预防COVID-19方面异常有效，但数据尚不能告诉我们它们是否阻止导致该病的病毒SARS-CoV-2的传播。
  免疫接种是否可以防止受感染者生病和感染其他人的问题并非当前的流行病所独有。据麦克马斯特大学病理学和分子医学教授Dawn Bowdish称，这种所谓的杀菌免疫力是消除天花的关键因素。
  天花病毒或天花病毒肆虐人类数千年，这在3000年历史的埃及法老拉美西斯五世的木乃伊中发现了脓疱痕迹即可证明。报告显示，该病毒在公元一世纪之前就已在全球蔓延。最终成为一个世纪以来的大流行病。历史学家认为，这种疾病在1900年至1980年正式消灭之间杀死了3亿多人。世界卫生组织麻疹和风疹技术顾问。
  抗天花的斗争激发了早期的接种努力，并导致英国医生爱德华·詹纳（Edward Jenner）于1796年研制出针对天花的牛痘疫苗。您产生的抗体，产生的反应会彻底清除系统中的病毒，” Bowdish说。
  尽管当今广泛使用的许多疫苗（例如针对麻疹的疫苗）都能产生非常有效的杀菌免疫力，但其他疫苗（例如乙肝疫苗）却不能。使用这些疫苗，可以训练一个人的免疫系统来预防疾病，但是病原体可以保留在该人体内，从而可能感染其他人。 Bowdish解释说，缺乏灭菌免疫力意味着该病原体会继续在人群中传播，从而可能在未接种疫苗的脆弱人群中引起疾病​​或进化为逃避我们的免疫反应。
  消毒免疫对于COVID-19疫苗制造商来说可能是一个崇高的目标，尽管对于遏制疾病不是必需的。据克劳克罗夫特（Crowcroft）说，这种免疫力的概念是细微的。她说：“实际上，保护范围最好是预防接种在何种程度上阻止了野生型病毒或细菌的传播。”
  轮状病毒的病情很简单，这种病会引起严重的呕吐和水样腹泻，对婴幼儿尤其危险。疫苗接种限制但不阻止病原体的复制。因此，它不能预防轻度疾病。但是，通过减少感染者的病毒载量，它可以减少传播，从而提供实质性的间接保护。根据美国疾病控制中心（Centers for Disease Control）的数据，在2006年在美国推出轮状病毒疫苗后的四到十年，对该疾病的阳性检测数量下降了74％至90％。

COVID-19 vaccine rollouts are finally upon us. They hope that  herd immunity—protection from an infectious disease that occurs once a sufficient proportion of the population has been vaccinated or infected—is on the horizon. But even though the  first vaccines to receive emergency use authorization from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration are exceptionally effective at preventing COVID-19,  data  cannot  yet tell us if they hinder  transmission of SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes the disease.
  The question of whether immunization prevents recipients from becoming ill  and from infecting others is not unique to the current pandemic. According to  Dawn Bowdish, a professor of pathology and molecular medicine at McMaster University, this so-called sterilizing immunity was a key factor in eliminating  smallpox.
  The smallpox, or variola, virus ravaged human populations for thousands of years,  evidenced by traces of pustules found on the 3,000-year-old mummy of Egyptian pharaoh Ramses V.   Reports indicate the virus was spreading globally by the first century C.E., and it eventually became a centuries-long pandemic. Historians think the disease killed more than  300 million people between 1900 and its official eradication in  1980. “Smallpox changed the history of the world—changing royal successions and the outcomes of wars that affected the destinies of whole countries,” says  Natasha Crowcroft, senior technical adviser for measles and rubella at the World Health Organization.
  The fight against smallpox inspired early inoculation efforts and led to English physician Edward Jenner’s cowpox  vaccine against smallpox in 1796. “The [smallpox] vaccination caused sterilizing immunity, meaning that you don’t carry any of the virus. The antibodies that you generate, the responses you generate, clear the virus from your system entirely,” Bowdish says.
  Although many vaccines widely used today (against  measles, for example) produce very effective sterilizing immunity,  others, such as the  hepatitis B vaccine, do not. With these vaccines, an individual’s immune system is trained to prevent illness, yet the pathogen can persist in that person’s body, potentially allowing them to infect others. A lack of sterilizing immunity means that the pathogen can continue to circulate in a population, where it may cause illness in unvaccinated and vulnerable people or evolve to evade our immune responses, Bowdish explains.
  Sterilizing immunity may have been a  lofty goal for COVID-19 vaccine manufacturers, though not necessary to curb disease. According to Crowcroft, the very concept of such immunity is nuanced. “In reality, the spectrum of protection might best be framed as the  extent to which vaccination prevents transmission of the wild-type virus or bacteria,” she says.
  The case of  rotavirus—which causes severe vomiting and watery diarrhea and is especially dangerous to infants and young children—is fairly straightforward. Vaccination limits, but does not stop, the pathogen from replicating. As such, it does not protect against mild disease. By reducing an infected person’s viral load, however, it decreases transmission, providing substantial indirect protection. According to the Centers for Disease Control, four to 10 years after the 2006 introduction of a rotavirus vaccine in the U.S., the number of positive tests for the disease fell by as much as  74 to 90 percent .
  The pathway to vaccine-mediated control of an infectious disease is not always so direct. Ultimately, whether, and to what degree, inoculation prevents transmission depends on the pathogen itself, the host or hosts it infects and the interaction between the two, Bowdish says.
  For example, vaccines against  Bordetella pertussis, the primary  bacterium that causes whooping cough, or pertussis, do a great job of preventing illness but do not entirely  clear the pathogen. Rather, as  B. pertussis replicates in the upper respiratory tract, vaccine-induced antibodies apply pressure via natural selection to weed out bacteria whose disease-causing genes are turned on. Because these same genes are responsible for the parts of the microorganisms that are targeted by antibodies, bacteria that keep them turned off evade the immune response and hang out undetected in the upper respiratory tract, Bowdish explains. This becomes a problem when someone with a naive immune system, such as an infant, contracts the pathogen. In the absence of antibodies,  B. pertussis’s disease-causing genes become activated again, causing illness. Nevertheless, the  introduction of pertussis vaccines in the 1940s cut annual U.S. cases from more than 100,000 to fewer than 10,000 by 1965. In the 1980s cases began slowly climbing again as parents increasingly  refused to vaccinate their children. Today there is renewed focus on reducing the chance of exposure and getting antibodies to infants by immunizing  pregnant women and new mothers.
  Efforts to arrest the spread of polio provide additional insight into the complexity of stopping an epidemic. The two main  categories of inoculations against polioviruses confer different types of immunity. The inactivated polio vaccine (IPV) protects against systemic infection and consequent paralysis but does not stop viral replication in the gut, so it offers no indirect protection to unvaccinated individuals. The oral polio vaccine (OPV) generates localized intestinal immunity, preventing infection and protecting against disease and transmission. Because the OPV uses a weakened live poliovirus, however, in rare cases among  underimmunized populations, the attenuated virus mutates, circulates and once again causes illness.  The Global Polio Eradication Initiative and the  World Health Organization recommend distinct vaccination strategies depending on the local context. In places where wild polio still exists, OPV is key to slowing transmission. In areas where the wild virus has been eradicated, IPV keeps populations protected. Thanks to widespread immunization programs, the U.S. has been polio-free since  1979, and the disease is on the verge of global  eradication.
  In a paper in the October 2020 issue of the  American Journal of Preventive Medicine, researchers  modeled what a COVID-19 vaccine with varying types of protection could mean. They found that if a vaccine protects 80 percent of those immunized and 75 percent of the population is vaccinated, it could largely end an epidemic without other measures such as social distancing. “Otherwise, you won’t be able to rely on the vaccine to return us to ‘normal,’” says  Bruce Y. Lee, a co-author of the paper and a professor at the CUNY Graduate School of Public Health and Health Policy. That is, if the vaccine only prevents disease or reduces viral shedding rather than eliminating it, additional public health measures may still be necessary. Even so, Lee stressed that a widespread nonsterilizing vaccine could still reduce burden on the health care system and save lives.
  Influenza may provide the best blueprint of what to expect going forward. The  most common flu vaccine—the inactivated virus—is not “truly sterilizing because it doesn’t generate local immune response in the respiratory tract,” Crowcroft says. This fact, coupled with low immunization rates (often  shy of 50 percent among adults) and the influenza virus’s ability to  infect and move between multiple species, enables it to constantly change in ways that make it hard for our immune system to recognize. Still, depending on the year, flu vaccines have been shown to  reduce hospitalizations  among older adults by an estimated 40 percent and intensive care admissions of all adults by as much as 82 percent.
  Research on  seasonal  coronaviruses suggests that SARS-CoV-2 could similarly evolve to evade our immune systems and vaccination efforts, though probably at a slower pace. And data remain mixed on the relationship between  symptoms,  viral load and  infectiousness. But ample precedent points to vaccines driving successful containment of infectious diseases even when they do not provide perfectly sterilizing immunity. “Measles, diphtheria, pertussis, polio, hepatitis B—these are all epidemic-prone diseases,” Crowcroft says. “They show that we don’t need 100 percent effectiveness at reducing transmission, or 100 percent coverage or 100 percent effectiveness against disease to triumph over infectious diseases.”
  Read more about the coronavirus outbreak from Scientific American  here. And read coverage from our international network of magazines  here.
